events.description: A small burden leaves a quiet hill. Three figures slip away at dusk, carrying a weight the world does not yet feel.
A shadow on horseback haunts the green lanes, sniffing the air for something it cannot name, and a creeping dread settles over every wood and hill it passes.
From a dark land in the east, nine shadows are loosed upon the world beneath a swollen moon, hunting a single small thing across the wide northern wilds.
Beneath ancient and ill-tempered trees, a drowsy song wells up to snare the travelers, until a merry old master of wood and water sings louder and breaks the spell.
Among cold green mounds, a chill spirit of the old dead seizes the wanderers, until a singing voice from the wild breaks the barrow open.
Under a moonless sky, in a crowded inn at a crossroads town, a weathered stranger watches from the shadows, and offers his sword to those who do not yet trust it.
On a lonely hilltop the shadows close in, their dread waxing in the dark, and a cruel cold blade finds its mark in the bearer of the burden.
At a river's edge the hunted one is run to ground, but the waters rise in fury and sweep the nine shadows away in a flood of foam and horses.
In a hidden valley of the elves, the wise and the proud gather in secret, and choose a handful of walkers, sending swift word out under the starlight to carry doom into the dark.
Far away beneath a cold moon, a lidless gaze turns north. Whispers of a small green country and its little folk seep at last into the deep places of the dark tower.
A poisoned whisper bends an old king toward sleep and a grey gloom settles over his halls, while behind a ring of stone a fallen wizard kindles furnaces and forges open war.
Out of the last homely valley, nine companions step into the cold beneath hard bright stars, set against nine shadows, and the road south swallows them.
The walkers come to a holly-clad country, quiet now, where ring-craft once flourished and only ruins remain, while flocks of black ravens wheel and watch and carry word to unseen masters.
A cruel red mountain wakes against them, hurling snow and stone until the company is driven back, beaten, from the high cold pass.
Before a hidden door in the mountain's roots, a blinding fog gathers and fell wolves howl within it, and a wretched creeping thing begins to follow the bearer unseen.
Deep beneath the mountains, on a narrow span over the abyss, a grey pilgrim stands against an ancient flame of shadow, and both plunge into the dark as a deeper shadow spreads across the world above.
Upon a lonely snowbound peak the long pursuit ends in fire and lightning; the demon is thrown down, and the pilgrim's life flickers out.
Among golden trees and silver lamps, where bright flowers bloom and weary hearts are healed, the grieving company is welcomed by a fair and ageless lady whose eyes see far more than they show.
The travelers slip away down a grey river, laden with quiet gifts from the golden wood, the last refuge dwindling behind them like a far star fading into the west.
At a muddy river crossing the horse-lords are broken, and the old king's only son falls beneath the black-handed host out of the stone ring.
The company is sundered as long shadows stretch across the great river. A proud captain falls defending the small ones, and the bearer and his faithful friend turn east into the wild alone.
Beneath a cloudless sky, raiders of the white hand carry off two captive halflings, mistaking small folk for the prize they seek.
In the dawn of the third day, a figure thought lost returns clad in white, and finds three weary hunters in the eaves of an ancient forest.
The river crossing falls a second time. The defenders of the plains are swept away as the waters rise in wrath, and the road to the mountain refuge lies open to the foe.
Through a night of rain and thunder, a vast host hurls itself against an old mountain keep, and the wall holds until the dawn turns the tide.
The shepherds of the trees rise in wrath, tear down the ring of stone, and loose a river to drown the pits and fires within.
A honeyed voice pleads from a ruined tower and is at last cast down and stripped of power, as a dark seeing-stone is hurled from the heights and a foul corruption seeps from the drowned pits below.
A morning comes with no sunrise, a creeping gloom flowing from the east, while the heir of old kings dares a haunted road beneath the mountains and the unquiet dead rise to follow at his call.
From a dead city in the eastern valley a pale lord leads forth his army, and unnatural night marches before them like a closing door.
In a distant forest, a dark fortress arises, menacing the sacred lands of the elves around and covering all in smoke and corruption.

High in a black pass the bearer falls into cruel hands, yet a small clear light still shines in the dark places, and his faithful friend, alone and unseen, takes up the burden and goes on into the dark land.
The dark host crosses the great river and rings the white city with fire, and a deathly dread rolls out ahead of it until the very air burns red and hope wavers behind the walls.
On the fields before the white city, the old horse-king falls at dawn, and the pale lord no living man could slay is undone by a maiden and a halfling.
Far from the southern war, the restless east stirs and pours out; the dark powers of forest and plain fall upon the woodland realms and the men of the long lake, and the north burns too.
A small army of the free peoples marches openly toward the dark gate, a thin light breaking through the cloud above them, knowing it cannot win and hoping only to draw the enemy's gaze.
The great enemy is deceived. His gaze fixes upon the army at his gate, and never falls upon two small figures crawling through the ash behind him.
At the heart of the burning mountain, the long-borne burden is at last unmade in fire, and a shadow that lay upon the whole world lifts and is gone.
With its power undone, the dark tower trembles and crumbles into dust, and the great enemy, formless at last, is scattered upon a rushing wind.
